title: Whether we owe care to the buildings we leave behind
summary: Argue whether communities and individuals have an ethical obligation to maintain the built environment they no longer actively use.
topic: Whether we owe care to the buildings we leave behind
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring whether people and communities owe a duty of care to buildings they have abandoned — mills, schools, hospitals, churches — or whether the cost and effort of preservation are simply too great to demand. Use a cleft sentence, a concession, at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include a specific geographic example (e.g. the former mill towns of northern England, or the dying industrial cities of the American Rust Belt). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a derelict mill in West Yorkshire communicates is not simply structural decay but the…
Granted, the costs of maintaining a building that serves no current function can be…
Left to deteriorate for decades, the Ancoats mills in Manchester stood as…
It is not the building itself that we owe a duty to, but the community memory that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: công trình được xếp hạng di sản (Anh)
rơi vào tình trạng xuống cấp
tái sử dụng thích ứng (chuyển đổi mục đích công trình cũ)
được tái sử dụng / chuyển đổi mục đích
vai trò trông coi / quản lý tài sản chung

hintVocab.phrase: to bear witness to a past
hintVocab.meaningVi: làm chứng nhân cho một quá khứ
lịch sử được hiện thân trong vật chất
trách nhiệm công dân / cộng đồng
ưu tiên sự tái sinh / tái phát triển
môi trường xây dựng / không gian nhân tạo

sampleText:
In the former mill towns of northern England — Hebden Bridge, Saltaire, parts of Manchester's Ancoats district — the question of what to do with buildings no longer wanted has been answered in sharply different ways. Some mills have been transformed into flats, galleries, and restaurants, their brick facades preserved while their interiors were entirely remade. Others have been left to fall into disrepair, their roofs caving in slowly, their floors colonised by pigeons and rising damp. The difference between these two outcomes has rarely been a matter of architectural significance. It has more often been a matter of money, political will, and whether anyone bothered to fight.

What a derelict building communicates is not simply its own structural failure. It communicates an indifference — on the part of owners, developers, and local authorities — to the embodied history that building contains. A Victorian mill that has been allowed to decay is not just a ruined structure; it is the site where a community's working life was organised for a century, and its deterioration erases something that cannot be reconstructed from a description. Collective memory requires physical anchors. When those anchors are removed — or left to rot — the memory they held becomes inaccessible to the people who inherit the place.

Granted, the costs of maintaining a building that serves no current economic function can be prohibitive. Preservation orders are frequently experienced by owners as unfunded mandates — obligations imposed without the resources needed to meet them. It would be unreasonable to demand that a private owner subsidise, indefinitely, an asset that produces no income simply because it is old and locally significant.

Even so, the principle of stewardship over the built environment is not primarily a financial argument — it is a civic one. Societies regularly spend money on things whose value is not reducible to economic output: public parks, libraries, memorials. The case for preserving significant old buildings belongs in this category. Having invested its labour and its identity in a place for generations, a community has a reasonable claim to expect that the physical record of that investment will not simply be abandoned when it is no longer profitable.

The most durable answer, demonstrated by Ancoats and by cities like Leipzig and Łódź that successfully adapted their industrial stock, is adaptive reuse: letting buildings change their purpose while keeping their fabric. This is not nostalgia. It is an acknowledgement that the built environment carries information that no photograph or archive fully replaces, and that the cost of losing it is paid not by the developer who walks away, but by every generation that comes after.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to reframe what a building's decay really means
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a derelict building communicates is not simply its own structural failure' dùng cấu trúc 'What … communicates is …' để đặt vấn đề thực sự — sự thờ ơ đối với lịch sử được hiện thân — vào vị trí nhấn mạnh cuối câu. Bắt đầu câu bằng 'A derelict building communicates more than structural failure' sẽ ít tạo ấn tượng hơn nhiều.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a derelict building communicates is not simply its own structural failure' uses a what-cleft to place the real issue — indifference to embodied history — in the emphatic position at the end. Beginning with 'A derelict building communicates more than structural failure' would be far less striking.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause to establish prior claim
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having invested its labour and its identity in a place for generations, a community has a reasonable claim' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành để xác lập hành động đã hoàn thành (đầu tư) trước khi quyền lợi được nêu (quyền yêu cầu). Trật tự thời gian này — trước tiên đầu tư, sau đó yêu cầu — là quan trọng đối với lập luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having invested its labour and its identity in a place for generations, a community has a reasonable claim' uses a perfect participle clause to establish the prior completed action (investment) before the entitlement is named (claim). This temporal sequence — investment first, claim second — is central to the argument.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở các thị trấn nhà máy cũ ở miền bắc nước Anh — Hebden Bridge, Saltaire, các phần của khu Ancoats ở Manchester — câu hỏi về việc phải làm gì với những tòa nhà không còn được muốn đã được trả lời theo những cách rất khác nhau. Một số nhà máy đã được chuyển đổi thành căn hộ, phòng trưng bày, và nhà hàng, mặt tiền gạch được bảo tồn trong khi nội thất bên trong hoàn toàn được làm lại. Những cái khác bị bỏ lại để rơi vào tình trạng xuống cấp, mái của chúng từ từ sụp đổ, sàn của chúng bị chim bồ câu và độ ẩm xâm chiếm. Sự khác biệt giữa hai kết quả này hiếm khi là vấn đề về ý nghĩa kiến trúc. Nó thường là vấn đề tiền bạc, ý chí chính trị, và liệu có ai quan tâm để chiến đấu hay không.

Điều một tòa nhà bỏ hoang truyền đạt không chỉ đơn giản là sự thất bại cấu trúc của chính nó. Nó truyền đạt sự thờ ơ — về phía chủ sở hữu, nhà phát triển, và chính quyền địa phương — đối với lịch sử được hiện thân trong tòa nhà đó. Một nhà máy thời Victoria đã bị để cho mục nát không chỉ là một cấu trúc bị hỏng; đó là nơi mà cuộc sống lao động của một cộng đồng được tổ chức trong một thế kỷ, và sự xuống cấp của nó xóa bỏ điều gì đó không thể được tái tạo từ một mô tả. Ký ức tập thể đòi hỏi những điểm neo vật chất. Khi những điểm neo đó bị loại bỏ — hoặc bị để mục nát — ký ức mà chúng giữ trở nên không thể tiếp cận với những người kế thừa nơi đó.

Thừa nhận, chi phí duy trì một tòa nhà không phục vụ bất kỳ chức năng kinh tế hiện tại nào có thể là quá lớn. Các lệnh bảo tồn thường được chủ sở hữu trải nghiệm như những nghĩa vụ không được tài trợ — nghĩa vụ được áp đặt mà không có nguồn lực cần thiết để đáp ứng chúng. Sẽ là vô lý khi yêu cầu một chủ sở hữu tư nhân trợ cấp, vô thời hạn, một tài sản không tạo ra thu nhập đơn giản vì nó cũ và có ý nghĩa địa phương.

Dẫu vậy, nguyên tắc trông coi môi trường xây dựng không chủ yếu là một lập luận tài chính — đó là một lập luận công dân. Các xã hội thường xuyên chi tiền cho những thứ có giá trị không thể quy về sản lượng kinh tế: công viên công cộng, thư viện, đài tưởng niệm. Trường hợp bảo tồn những tòa nhà cũ quan trọng thuộc vào danh mục này. Sau khi đã đầu tư lao động và bản sắc của mình vào một nơi qua nhiều thế hệ, một cộng đồng có yêu cầu hợp lý để kỳ vọng rằng hồ sơ vật chất của khoản đầu tư đó sẽ không đơn giản bị bỏ lại khi nó không còn sinh lợi nữa.

Câu trả lời bền vững nhất, được chứng minh bởi Ancoats và bởi các thành phố như Leipzig và Łódź đã thích ứng thành công kho công trình công nghiệp của họ, là tái sử dụng thích ứng: để các tòa nhà thay đổi mục đích trong khi giữ lại cấu trúc của chúng. Đây không phải là sự hoài niệm. Đó là sự thừa nhận rằng môi trường xây dựng mang thông tin mà không có bức ảnh hay kho lưu trữ nào có thể thay thế hoàn toàn, và chi phí mất mát nó được trả không phải bởi nhà phát triển bỏ đi, mà bởi mọi thế hệ tiếp theo.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a what-cleft to reframe a building's decay as a social message?
options: What a derelict building communicates is not simply its own structural failure.
A derelict building what communicates is structural failure not simply.
It is what communicates derelict building not structural simply failure.
Not simply structural failure what a derelict building communicates is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What [noun phrase + verb] is [noun phrase]' is the correct what-cleft structure. The key claim is placed after 'is'.
mcQuestions.hint: The cleft structure: 'What [something] does is [the important thing]'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'adaptive reuse' mean in architecture?
options: Demolishing a building and constructing a modern replica
Converting an existing building to a new purpose while retaining its original structure
Moving a historic building to a different location
Restoring a building to its original condition and function
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines it as 'letting buildings change their purpose while keeping their fabric' — the structure is retained but the use changes.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about both words: what is being 'adapted' and what is being 'reused'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a perfect participle clause to establish a prior claim?
options: Having invested its labour and its identity in a place for generations, a community has a reasonable claim.
A community invested its labour and identity having for generations a reasonable claim.
For generations invested its labour having identity a community claim reasonable.
Generations having a community invested identity labour in a reasonable claim place.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having + past participle' establishes a prior completed action; the main clause ('a community has a reasonable claim') follows directly.
mcQuestions.hint: The 'having' clause must precede the main clause and share the same subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what is done to historic buildings?
options: Local authorities allow mills to decay.
Mills have been allowed to decay by local authorities.
Decay local authorities mills allowing been have.
Local authorities have been allowed decay mills.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Mills have been allowed to decay' places 'mills' in subject position, foregrounding the buildings as the thing affected rather than the decision-makers.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option makes 'mills' the grammatical subject of the sentence?

options: The sum of individual memories stored in a digital database
A shared sense of a group's past, maintained through stories, places, and cultural practices
The memory of a specific person who has been designated as a community historian
A form of group therapy used to process traumatic events
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Collective memory' refers to the shared understanding of the past that a group holds together — not the sum of individual memories but a socially maintained narrative.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about how communities — not individuals — 'remember' their shared past.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly introduces a concession about the cost of preservation?
options: Granted, the costs of maintaining a building that serves no current economic function can be prohibitive.
Granted costs maintaining no current economic function building prohibitive can be.
Prohibitive costs building maintaining granted no current economic function.
The costs are granted no current economic function maintaining building prohibitive.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, [complete clause]' is the correct concession structure; 'prohibitive' means so high as to prevent action.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Granted,' there should be a full independent clause with a clear subject and verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'derelict' mean when describing a building?
options: Recently renovated but not yet occupied
Protected by a legal preservation order
Abandoned and in a state of disrepair
Scheduled for demolition next year
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Derelict' describes a place that has been abandoned and has fallen into a poor state of repair through neglect.
mcQuestions.hint: The word describes both the state and the cause — what happens to a building when it is left untended?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'stewardship' mean in the context of this essay?
options: Legal ownership of a listed building
The responsible care and management of something held in trust for others
A government grant given to heritage site owners
The academic study of architectural history
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stewardship' implies managing something not just for your own benefit, but on behalf of a broader group or future generations.
mcQuestions.hint: A steward looks after something on behalf of others — who are the 'others' here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'fall into disrepair' correctly?
options: Others have been left to fall into disrepair, their roofs caving in slowly.
Their roofs into disrepair falling slowly caving left have been.
Falling disrepair others slowly their roofs caving into left been.
Others slowly have been left disrepair their roofs into falling caving.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Fall into disrepair' is a fixed phrase meaning to gradually deteriorate; the participial phrase 'their roofs caving in slowly' adds descriptive detail.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Fall into disrepair' is a fixed expression — it should appear together in the correct order.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'embodied history' mean in the context of buildings?
options: History that has been written on a building's walls as a display
History that is physically present in the material and form of a building itself
A history book commissioned when a building was constructed
History that is associated with the bodies buried near a building
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Embodied' means physically present in the body or material of something; a building's 'embodied history' is the history held in its fabric, form, and site.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Embody' means to give physical form to something — what physical form does history take in a building?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains formal register throughout?
options: It's such a shame when old buildings get knocked down because they're part of our history.
The demolition of historically significant structures results in the irretrievable loss of embodied collective memory.
Old buildings matter a lot and tearing them down is kind of wrong honestly.
Like, who decides what buildings get saved and which ones just get torn down?
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Demolition of historically significant structures', 'irretrievable loss', and 'embodied collective memory' are all precise, formal academic collocations.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, intensifiers like 'such a shame', and informal markers like 'like' and 'honestly'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does it mean for a cost to be 'prohibitive'?
options: So low that it makes the project very easy to fund
Difficult to calculate in advance
So high that it effectively prevents the action from being taken
Spread evenly across a large number of contributors
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Prohibitive' comes from 'prohibit' — a prohibitive cost is one so high that it acts as a prohibition in practice.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the verb 'prohibit' — what does it mean to 'prohibit' something?

options: Intensive urban development that displaces long-term residents
A disease affecting trees in urban parks
The physical and social deterioration of an urban area, often visible in abandoned buildings and declining infrastructure
Excessive car traffic causing pollution in city centres
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Urban blight' describes the spread of physical decay and social decline across a city district, often associated with deindustrialisation or disinvestment.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Blight' in agriculture kills plants — what does it do to a city?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best introduces an argument that a civic principle outweighs a financial concern?
options: On the other hand, the financial costs are very large.
Even so, the principle of stewardship over the built environment is not primarily a financial argument — it is a civic one.
Therefore, preservation is clearly the cheapest option.
In addition, many buildings have historical value.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' pivots from the conceded financial concern back to the essay's main position, making the civic-financial contrast explicit.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option acknowledges the financial issue but clearly elevates a different principle?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is a 'preservation order' in the context of buildings?
options: A financial grant given to restore a historic building
A legal designation that prevents a building from being altered or demolished without permission
An agreement between a developer and a community group
An architectural survey of a building's historical features
mcQuestions.explanation: A preservation order (in the UK, a 'listed building consent' is required for changes) legally protects a building from demolition or unsympathetic alteration.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'preserve' means and how a legal 'order' enforces it.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the cost of losing it is paid … by every generation that comes after' mean?
options: Future generations will have to pay financially to rebuild demolished buildings
Future generations inherit the cultural and historical loss caused by demolished or neglected buildings
Future generations are legally responsible for the debts of developers who demolished buildings
Future generations will be charged entrance fees to visit heritage sites
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'cost' here is not financial but cultural — future generations lose access to embodied history and collective memory when significant buildings are destroyed.
mcQuestions.hint: What kind of 'cost' has been described throughout the essay — financial or something else?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a building in your city or town that has been demolished, abandoned, or converted in a way that felt like a loss? Who made that decision, and do you think the community had enough say in it?
